---
title: "More on classes"
# subtitle: "More on classes"
author: |
    Khiem Nguyen 
    <br>Lecturer in Multiscale Materials
    <br><khiem.nguyen@glasgow.ac.uk>
author-meta: "Lecturer in Multiscale Materials"
format: 
    revealjs:
        theme: simple   # other themes: beige, blood, dark, dracula, league, moon, night, serif, simple, sky, solarized
        mouse-wheel: true
        highlight-style: github
        slide-number: true
        title-slide-style: pandoc
        echo: true
        code-overflow: wrap
        code-line-numbers: true
        code-block-bg: false          # <-- remove white background in code blocks
        # code-block-border-left: false # optional: remove left border
        # highlight-style: monokai      # good for dark images
        toc: true
        toc-depth: 1
        toc-title: Presentation Outline
        chalkboard: true
        css: simple.css
---

# The hidden `this` pointer

In Python, the first parameter of function member, normally named `self`, refer to the "current" object "under consideration".

```{.python}
class Fraction:
    def __init__(self, nominator, denominator):
        self.nominator = nominator
        self.denomintory = denominator
```

Now, we learn the hidden pointer `this` which acts like `self`, but it is a pointer.

## The hidden `this` pointer

&#9998;&nbsp; The keyword `this` is a const pointer that holds the address of the current implicit object.

::: {#lst-hidden-pointer-this lst-cap="Keyword `this` is a const pointer that holds the address of the current implicit object. Filename=`hidden_pointer_this.cpp`"}
```{.cpp}
{{< include ./cpp/hidden_pointer_this.cpp >}}
```
:::
&#9998;&nbsp; We use `->` to select a member from a pointer to an object.

## How is `this` set?

When we make the function call
  
```{.cpp}
simple.set_value(42);
```
the compiler rewrites the expression `simple.set_value(42)` as

```{.cpp}
// This is hypothetical explanation. 
// This line of code won't run in the main function.
Simple::set_value(&simple, 42); 
```

## `this` always pointers to the object being operated on

&#9998;&nbsp; Just like `self` variable in Python, each member function has a single `this` pointer parameter that points to the implicit object.

```{.cpp}
int main()
{
    Simple a{42};       // this = &a inside the Simple constructor
    Simple b{42};       // this = &b inside the Simple constructor
    a.set_value(12);    // this = &a inside member function set_value()
    b.set_value(21);    // this = &b inside member function set_value()

    return 0;
}
```

## Explicitly referencing `this`

- Most of the time, we don't need to explicitly reference the `this` pointer.
- If we have a parameter with the same name as a data member, we can disambiguate them by using `this`.

    ```{.cpp}
    struct Something
    {
        double data{}; // not using m_ prefix because this is a struct

        void set_data(int data)
        {
            this->data = data;  // this->data is the member, 
                                // data is the local parameter
        }
    };
    ```

## Returning `*this`: function chaining

&#9998;&nbsp; **Why we want to return** `*this` -- object pointed by `this`

- It can sometimes be useful to have a member function return the implicit object as a return value.
- This allows member functions to be [*chained together*]{style="color:blue"}.
- This is called [**function chaining**]{style="color:red"} (or [**method chaining**]{style="color:red"}).

&#9998;&nbsp; **Common example we have used all the time**:

- Consider:

    ```{.cpp}
    std::cout << "Hello " << user_name;
    ```

- The compiler evaluates the above snippet like this:
  
    ```{.cpp}
    (std::cout << "Hello, ") << user_name;
    ```

- The expression `(std::cout << "Hello, ")` returns `std::cout`. 
- Then, `std::cout` is used again in `std::cout << user_name`.

&#10149;&nbsp; We will revisit this in the lesson "overloading operators".

## Returning `*this*`: function chaining

&#10149;&nbsp; Let us consider the example:

::: {#lst-hidden-pointer-this lst-cap="Return `*this` to allow method chaining. Filename=`method_chaining_example.cpp`"}
```{.cpp}
{{< include ./cpp/method_chaining_example.cpp >}}
```
:::

# Introduction to destructors

&#9998;&nbsp; We covered [**constructors**]{style="color:red"}, which are special member functions that are called when an object of a non-aggregate class type is created.

&#9998;&nbsp; Analogously, classes have another type of special member function that is called automatically when an object of a non-aggregate class type is destroyed: [**destructors**]{style="color:red"}

## The cleanup problem

&#10149;&nbsp; Let us look at this not-so-hypothetical situation

::: {#lst-database-helper lst-cap="The memory leak occurs because we did not clean up the data pointed by the pointer `connection_string`. Filename=`database_helper_without_destructor.cpp`"}
```{.cpp}
{{< include ./cpp/database_helper_without_destructor.cpp >}}
```
:::

&#10149;&nbsp; Memory leak occured. We did not free up the memory space pointed by `connection_string` -- Look at the code line `connection_string = new std::string(path)`.

## The cleanup problem

&#10149;&nbsp; We can delete the memory pointed by `connection_string` by writing a public function (we cannot access the private member `connection_string` in `main()`)

```{.cpp}
class DatabaseHelper {
private:
    std::string* connection_string;

public:
    // yada yada as before
    void cleanup() {
        delete connection_string;
    }
};

int main ()
{
    {
        // Object created inside a block (scope starts)
        DatabaseHelper db("127.0.0.1:5432/my_database");
        db.query("SELECT * FROM users;");
        // Cleanup: Return the memory to the OS before it is destroyed.
        db.cleanup();
    } // <--- SCOPE ENDS HERE
    std::cout << "The object is gone, connection is closed and memory is returned to OS." << std::endl;
}
```

## The cleanup problem: A specific example

&#10149;&nbsp; Let us run the following code snippet.

::: {#lst-array-example lst-cap="In this example a Vector object is managed by a dynamically allocated array. Filename=`array_without_destructor.cpp`"}
```{.cpp}
{{< include ./cpp/array_without_destructor.cpp >}}
```
:::

&#10149;&nbsp; What about we forget the little line of code `vector.cleanup()` because it is written in a much more complex logic \& code?

## Destructors to rescue

&#9998;&nbsp; A class [**Destructor**]{style="color:red"} is called automatically when an object of a non-aggregate class type is destroyed.

&#9998;&nbsp; [**Destructors**]{style="color:red"} are designed to allow a class to do any necessary clean up before an object of the class is destroyed.

&#9998;&nbsp; A class can only have [**a single destructor**]{style="color:blue"}.

### Destructor naming

- The destructor must have the same name as the class, preceded by a tilde (`~`).
- The destructor cannot take arguments.
- The destructor has no return type.

```{.cpp}
Simple(int id) : id { id }      // constructor
{
    std::cout << "Constructing Simple " << id << '\n';
}

~Simple() // here's our destructor
{
    std::cout << "Destructing Simple " << di << '\n';
}
```

## Destructors: An example

::: {#lst-destructor-example lst-cap="An simple example of destructor. Filename=`destructor_example.cpp`"}
```{.cpp}
{{< include ./cpp/destructor_example.cpp >}}
```
:::

## An implicit destructor

Again, not a surprising:

- f a non-aggregate class type object has no user-declared destructor, the compiler will generate a destructor, called [**implicit destructor**]{style="color:red"}, with an empty body.
- It is effectively just a placeholder.
- If our class does [not need to do any cleanup on destruction]{style="color:blue"}, it is [fine to not define a destructor at all]{style="color:blue"}. The compiler generates an implicit destructor.

## Revisit `Vector` example

&#10149;&nbsp; Here we go and we know what to do with `Vector` defined above

::: {#lst-array-with-destructor lst-cap="Let the destructor do the memory cleanup. Filename=`array_with_destructor.cpp`"}
```{.cpp}
template <typename T, int N>    // This is a good opportunity to teach this
class Vector
{
    T* data;    // pointer to the data type T
public:
    Vector() {
        data = new T[N];
    }
    ~Vector() {
        delete data;
        std::cout << "Array pointed by pointer " << data 
                  << " is cleaned and returned to OS\n";
    }
    // rest the same
}
```
:::

# Class Template

- Just like when we learn _function template_, a similar problem arises in classes too:
  
    ```{.cpp}
    struct Pair {
        int first {};
        int second {};
    };
    struct Pair {
        double first {};
        double second {};
    };
    ```
- In fact, we have used `std::vector<double>` in the assignments.

## Class template: Rationale

&#10149;&nbsp; Look at this scenario:

- We define a `Pair`:
  
    ```{.cpp}
    struct Pair
    {
        int first {};
        int second {};
    }
    ```

- Soon we recognize that we need a `Pair` with `double` data and possibly `std::vector<double>` data.
  
    ```{.cpp}
    struct Pair
    {
        std::vector<double> first {};
        std::vector<double> second {};
    }
    ```

&#10149;&nbsp; That's enough to see the problem we encountered just like how we did with functions.

&#10149;&nbsp; We need a **template** to define class type (`struct` and `class`) so that [_we can instantiate different classes depending on their use cases._]{style="color:blue"}


## Class template


::: {.my-box}
A [**class template**]{style="color:red"} is a template definition for instantiating class types.
:::

::: {#lst-pair-class-template lst-cap="An example of class template for `Pair` Filename=`pair_class_template.cpp`"}
```{.cpp}
{{< include ./cpp/pair_class_template.cpp >}}
```
:::

## Class template

Just like with function templates, we

- start a class template definition with a template parameter declaration
- begin with the `template` keyword
- specify all of the template types inside angled brackets `<>`
- use the keyword `typename` for
- continue to define class type (`struct` and `class`) as usual.

## Class template: Compiler's POV
&#10149;&nbsp; Same example as above, showing what the compiler actually compiles. We can run this code.

::: {#lst-pair-class-template lst-cap="Compiler's point of view of instantiation of class template: `Pair` example again. Filename=`pair_class_template_compiler_pov.cpp`"}
```{.cpp}
{{< include ./cpp/pair_class_template_compiler_pov.cpp >}}
```
:::

## Class template and function template: Put together in an example
&#10149;&nbsp; Let us put class template and function template all toghether for better understanding.

::: {#lst-class-template-and-func-template lst-cap="This example combines both class template and function template. Filename=`class_template_and_func_template.cpp`"}
```{.cpp}
{{< include ./cpp/class_template_and_func_template.cpp >}}
```
:::

## Class template and function template: Compiler's POV

When the `max()` function is called with a `Pair<int>` in the function call
```{.cpp}
max<int>(p1)
```
with `p1` is an `Pair<int>` object, the compiler will instantiate the function `int max<int>(Pair<int>)` looking like this
```{.cpp}
template <>
constexpr int max(Pair<int> p)
{
    return (p.first < p.second ? p.second : p.first);
}
```

## Class templates with multiple template types

&#9998;&nbsp; It is not a surprise that class templates can also have multiple template types.

::: {#lst-class-multiple-template-types lst-cap="An example of a class template with multiple template types. Filename=`class_multiple_template_types.cpp`"}
```{.cpp}
{{< include ./cpp/class_multiple_template_types.cpp >}}
```
:::

## Standard template library: `std::pair`

&#9998;&nbsp; Working with pairs of data is common, the C++ standard library contains a class template named `std::pair`.

&#9998;&nbsp; `std::pair` is defined identically to the `Pair` class template with multiple template types we've just learned.

# Friend non-member functions

- We've used `std::cout << some_object;` so many times without noticing that `std::cout` is an object, `<<` is an operator. 
- It works like `object1 + object2` in which 
  - `object1` is `std::cout`
  - `object2` is `some_object`
  - `<<` plays the role of operator `+`.
- If we write `std::cout << polynomial`, we wish `std::cout` can access private data members of `polynomial` to print meaning outputs to the consol.

&#10149;&nbsp; Here we go: we need the _magic_ of `friend`.

## Friendship is magic

- Inside the body of a class, a [**friend declaration**]{style="color:red"} (using the `friend` keyword) can be used to tell the compiler that some other class or function is now _a friend_.
- A [**friend**]{style="color:red"} is a class or function (member of other classes or non-member) that has been [**granted full access to private and protected members of another class**]{style="color:blue"} 

::: {.my-box}
**Key insight**

Friendship is always granted by the class whose members will be accessed (not by the class or function desiring access).
:::

## Friend non-member functions

A [**friend function**]{style="color:red"} is a function (member or non-member) that can access the private or protected members of a class as though it were a member of that class.

::: {#lst-friend-nonmember-function lst-cap="A non-member function can access data members of a class by being `friend` with that class. Filename=`friend_nonmember_function.cpp`"}
```{.cpp}
{{< include ./cpp/friend_nonmember_function.cpp >}}
```
:::
